13 min read

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Elevate on UnsplashIn this story, I covered the principles of software testing. However, these were unit tests, which are intended to test small components, such as functions and classes. When building an application, we have to write more complex tests that cover the integrations and overall behavior of our endpoints. The concept of different types of tests was also discussed in the linked story through the testing pyramid:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In this story, we will test a FastAPI application, which was developed in the following series of articles:

• HTTP protocol: a practical perspective with Python and Fast API

• HTTP Protocol: authentication and authorization

• Integrating an SQL database into a RESTful application
This application has the following endpoints, which we must cover in our tests:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Even though Python and FastAPI will be used in this article, we will cover concepts and practices applicable to any programming language and web framework.
Additionally, pytest will be used as the testing framework. If you read the previous Medium article about software testing, you should be familiar with its fundamentals.
Core principles
First of all, let’s remind ourselves of the principles of any testing code:

• Simplicity: testing code is not production code, so it must be straightforward to understand, almost serving as documentation. Use the AAA pattern discussed in the previous story as a template.

• Multiple scenarios: tests must cover success and error paths. We must ensure that the application succeeds and fails at the right moments.
Test skeleton
Like many test frameworks, FastAPI provides a test client to invoke the application endpoints without needing the application to be up. That way, we don’t need to start up our application before the tests and use a Python package, such as requests, to issue HTTP requests.
Let’s start by writing a test for the /health endpoint, a straightforward API that validates whether our application is responsive. This endpoint does not interact with our database, which will facilitate our work and serve as a 101 example:
# tests/test_health.py
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from starlette import statusfrom main import app
client = TestClient(app)
def test_health():
response = client.get("/health")
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK
assert response.json() == {"name": "linktree-clone"}
Notice how the test client is instantiated and used as a door to our application. If we run the test, the following output should be produced:
pytest .collected 1 item
test_health.py . [100%]
====================================================================================================== 1 passed in 0.10s =======================================================================================================
Pytest fixtures
Pytest has the context of fixtures. A fixture defines an entity used in the context of a test. Examples are:

• A user to be authenticated at testing time

• A database session to be used at testing time

• The FastAPI test client
Fixtures are passed as arguments to test functions. This indicates that the fixture must be issued before test execution (i.e., in the arrange phase of the test according to the AAA pattern) and that it can be manipulated in the test run.
To understand this concept, let’s create a fixture for the test client:
# tests/conftest.py
import pytest
from starlette.testclient import TestClientfrom main import app
@pytest.fixture(name="client")
def client_fixture():
client = TestClient(app)
yield client
Note that:

• The fixture is a value yielded by a function surrounded by the pytest.fixture decorator.

• Fixtures are commonly defined in a conftest.py file. This allows them to be shared by our tests across different test files.
Let’s replace the global variable client with the fixture from above:
# tests/test_health.py
from starlette import statusdef test_health(client):
response = client.get("/health")
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK
assert response.json() == {"name": "linktree-clone"}
Notice how the fixture is passed as an argument to the test function. If you re-run the tests, they should pass just like before.
Testing the database
At testing time, we could use the application database to store testing data. However, this would have some inconveniences:

• We would consume storage and computational resources due to our test runs. When querying data for analytics, we need to pay attention to the test data and ignore it in our queries. There are several strategies to handle this concern, such as keeping a separate database for tests or storing a column indicating whether the data comes from tests.

• Keeping test data between test runs creates some concerns. For example, in our data model, different users cannot share the same email address. If we have a test that creates a user, the user must either be deleted at the end, so the email is released, or use a random email at each execution. This may create some coupling between our test cases and runs, which is undesirable.
Consequently, it’s common to connect to a separate database in our tests. With the help of pytest fixtures and FastAPI, we can easily achieve that:
# tests/conftest.py
import pytest
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, StaticPool
from sqlmodel import SQLModel, Session
from starlette.testclient import TestClientfrom main import app
from model.inmem_storage.storage import get_db_session
@pytest.fixture(name="session")
def session_fixture():
engine = create_engine(
"sqlite://", connect_args={"check_same_thread": False}, poolclass=StaticPool
)
SQLModel.metadata.create_all(engine)
with Session(engine) as session:
yield session
@pytest.fixture(name="client")
def client_fixture(session: Session):
def get_session_override():
return session
app.dependency_overrides[get_db_session] = get_session_override
client = TestClient(app)
yield client
app.dependency_overrides.clear()
In the updated snippet:

• We added a session fixture to conftest.py. This fixture creates a database engine and a derived session exclusively for the tests.

• We provide the session fixture to the client_fixture function. This ensures that session_fixture is run before client_fixture.

• The client fixture overrides the get_db_session function with a get_session_override function that returns the test session.

• The MySQL database is overridden with a SQLite database at testing time. We could rather connect to another MySQL database. We opted for a SQLite database for simplicity since it is reset between test runs and, as a consequence, cleans up data.
Testing login
Now that we have the minimum we need, let’s start writing more complex tests! We will cover the login flow here. When writing tests, it’s good to start by enumerating relevant scenarios to cover. For login, they are:

• Successful login. A 200 Ok response code and the session ID must be returned.

• Log in with the wrong username. A 401 Unauthorized response code must be returned.

• Log in with the wrong password. A 401 Unauthorized response code must be returned.

• Log in without the username input. A 422 Unprocessable Content response code must be returned.

• Log in without the password input. A 422 Unprocessable Content response code must be returned.

• Log in without the username and password. A 422 Unprocessable Content response code must be returned.
The code covering such flows is:
import pytest
from starlette import statusdef test_login_random_credentials(client, faker):
signup_payload = {
"first_name": faker.first_name(),
"last_name": faker.last_name(),
"email": faker.email(),
"password": "Str0ngP@ss",
}
response = client.post("/signup", json=signup_payload)
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_201_CREATED
response = client.post("/login", data={
"username": signup_payload["email"],
"password": signup_payload["password"]
})
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK
assert "sid" in response.json()
def test_login_known_credentials(client, faker):
signup_payload = {
"first_name": faker.first_name(),
"last_name": faker.last_name(),
"email": "john.doe@test.com",
"password": "Str0ngP@ss",
}
response = client.post("/signup", json=signup_payload)
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_201_CREATED
response = client.post("/login", data={
"username": signup_payload["email"],
"password": signup_payload["password"]
})
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK
assert "sid" in response.json()
@pytest.mark.parametrize("login_payload", [
({
"username": "john.doe.2@test.com",
"password": "Str0ngP@ss"
}),
({
"username": "john.doe@test.com",
"password": "invalid-Str0ngP@ss"
}),
])
def test_login_with_wrong_credentials(client, faker, login_payload):
signup_payload = {
"first_name": faker.first_name(),
"last_name": faker.last_name(),
"email": "john.doe@test.com",
"password": "Str0ngP@ss",
}
response = client.post("/signup", json=signup_payload)
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_201_CREATED
response = client.post("/login", data=login_payload)
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED
assert response.json() == {
"detail": "Incorrect username or password"
}
@pytest.mark.parametrize("login_payload", [
({"username": "john.doe@test.com"}),
({"password": "Str0ngP@ss"}),
({}),
])
def test_login_with_missing_credential(client, faker, login_payload):
signup_payload = {
"first_name": faker.first_name(),
"last_name": faker.last_name(),
"email": "john.doe@test.com",
"password": "Str0ngP@ss",
}
response = client.post("/signup", json=signup_payload)
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_201_CREATED
response = client.post("/login", data=login_payload)
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_422_UNPROCESSABLE_CONTENT
Notice that:

• The Faker package was used to generate random test data. I introduced this package in my previous text about software testing. This package provides a fixture with a Faker class instance, which generates random data.

• We have two success flows, one with random email and password, and another with known, constant credentials.

• For some test flows, we had to call the signup API to create a user. This is essential for success cases since a user must have signed up to log in.

• To avoid code duplication, we used the pytest parametrize annotation, which allows us to pass parameters to our test functions as arguments. This was also discussed as a good practice in my previous article.
Testing signup
Among the user APIs, the signup endpoint is the key for users to get started with our application. Therefore, we will emphasize its tests. The following scenarios will be covered:

• Successful signup without specifying the ID. A 201 Created response code and the user's public attributes are expected.

• Successful signup with a specific ID. A 201 Created response code and the user’s public attributes are expected. The ID must match the value sent in the request body.

• Signup error due to an already-in-use ID. A 409 Conflict response code must be returned.

• Signup error due to an already-in-use email. A 409 Conflict response code must be returned.

• Signup error due to missing mandatory fields in the request payload. A 422 Unprocessable Content response code must be returned.

• Signup error due to an invalid email address. A 422 Unprocessable Content response code must be returned.

• Signup error due to an invalid password. A 422 Unprocessable Content response code must be returned.
The test code is:
# tests/test_signup.py
import pytest
from starlette import statusdef test_signup(client, faker):
signup_payload = {
"first_name": faker.first_name(),
"last_name": faker.last_name(),
"email": faker.email(),
"password": "Str0ngP@ss",
}
response = client.post("/signup", json=signup_payload)
assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_201_CREATED
respons
